title: The League Has an App Now
summary: That's right. A nineteen-year-old fantasy football league has its own app. Install it to your home screen and try to act surprised.
description: Let's address the elephant in the room: yes, a sixteen-team dynasty fantasy football league that has existed since 2007 now has its own dedicated app. Not a shared app. Not a link to MFL's mobile site that looks like it was designed for a Blackberry. An actual, installable, icon-on-your-home-screen app, sitting right there between Instagram and whatever meditation app you downloaded in January and opened once. The audacity is not lost on us.

Now, before you start drafting your acceptance speech for the App Store — it's not in the App Store. It's a Progressive Web App, which means you install it straight from your browser. On iPhone, hit the share button and tap "Add to Home Screen." On Android, tap the three-dot menu. On desktop, look for the install icon in Chrome's address bar. No downloads, no updates, no 47 permission requests asking for access to your contacts, camera, and existential dread. It just works.

Here's what the app does right now: it logs you in, remembers who you are for 90 days, defaults every roster page to your team, and highlights your franchise across the site. That's it. That's the app. We built an entire installable application so you could stop scrolling through a dropdown to find your own team. In the future, this is where lineup submissions, free agent bidding, and other MFL actions will live — but for now, it's a very fancy, very persistent bookmark that knows your name. Install it anyway. You know you want to.

title: Weekly Fixes & Polish (Feb 17-23)
summary: 20 bug fixes and style improvements across navigation & routing, rosters, rankings, design system & theming, and more.
description: This week's 20 bug fixes and polish improvements touched several areas of the site:

Navigation & Routing — URL rewriting now uses Astro middleware instead of Vercel rewrites for more reliable page routing. The /theleague path prefix is now hidden on theleague.us so URLs are cleaner (e.g., theleague.us/rosters instead of theleague.us/theleague/rosters). Mobile and tablet header now stacks nav icons below the logo for a tidier layout.
Rosters — Roster page now shows a random team for new visitors instead of always defaulting to the same team.

Rankings — Removed extra right padding from composite ranking columns for tighter alignment. Ranking column border styles refined for better visual separation between sources. Player matching for imported rankings now handles nicknames (e.g., Mike/Michael) for more accurate matches.

Design System & Theming — Ranking type badge colors softened while maintaining WCAG AA contrast. New --table-header-gradient design token unifies all table header styles across the site. Hardcoded fonts and icon sizes replaced with design tokens for consistency. Accent link hover color now uses brand green instead of a non-brand amber.
Import Rankings — Ranking type badges on import page now use solid colors for better readability. Import rankings cards now have white backgrounds instead of gray.
League Summary — League summary subtitle spacing tightened from 5rem to 2rem.

Free Agents — Free agent average rank column colors updated for better contrast. Average ranking column border made symmetrical on the Free Agents page. Defense player avatars now properly centered using flex alignment. Defense avatars are now circular with smaller, better-proportioned logos. Fixed empty free agents list caused by missing projection data by switching to projectedScores.
Homepage — Homepage card hover color corrected to use the green design token.

title: League Planner
summary: Your roster's Next Year tab now reshuffles itself based on where you are in the offseason — because what matters in January is not what matters in March.
description: The offseason isn't one season — it's four seasons wearing the same trench coat. Tagging window priorities look nothing like free agency priorities, which look nothing like draft night priorities. The old Next Year tab didn't care about any of that. It showed you the same static layout whether you were evaluating franchise tag candidates in January or scrambling for draft picks in April. The League Planner fixes this by rearranging itself around whatever phase of the offseason you're actually in.

After Week 10, extension and franchise tag tools rise to the top of the page because that's what matters before the tagging deadline. Once free agency opens, a Potential Targets card takes the lead — complete with player headshots, positions, and your imported ranking columns — surfacing the free agents your roster actually needs. Missing a top-8 quarterback or tight end? Only have one top-16 wide receiver? The Planner flags the gaps and recommends targets. During the draft, your 2026 and 2027 pick assets take center stage so you can plan trades without flipping between tabs.

It's the difference between a to-do list and a to-do list that knows what day it is. Open the Planner in February and it's thinking about tags. Open it in March and it's thinking about free agents. Open it in April and it's thinking about the draft. You should probably be thinking about those things too.

title: Extension Calculator Fix
summary: Veteran extension and franchise tag calculations now correctly use the previous season's frozen salary averages.
description: For a brief window after the February league year rollover, the extension and franchise tag calculators were doing their math with all the reliability of a rookie quarterback in a snow game. The problem? They were pulling salary averages from the new league year's roster pool — a pool where half the teams hadn't even finished assembling their rosters yet. The result was position averages that looked like they were generated by a random number generator rather than actual contract data.

The fix locks all extension and franchise tag calculations onto the previous season's week 14 frozen salary snapshot — the gold standard of salary data, captured when rosters are at their most complete and stable. This frozen data stays in play until the current season reaches its own week 14 freeze, ensuring you never get calculations based on incomplete information. If you used the Trade Builder, Rosters, or Contract Tools pages during the rollover window and something looked off, this is why — and it's now corrected.

title: My Rank Composite
summary: Pick your trusted ranking sources, assign weights, and see a single personalized "My Rank" column on the Free Agents page.
description: The arms race of ranking sources has officially reached the point where owners need a ranking of their rankings. With seven import sources available — and some owners hoarding all seven — the Free Agents page was turning into a spreadsheet that would make an accountant weep. Something had to give, and that something is the new My Rank composite.

Head to the Import Rankings page, check the boxes next to the sources you actually trust, and assign each a weight: 1x for sources you respect, 2x for sources you swear by, and 3x for whatever gospel you've decided is the absolute truth this offseason. Once you've selected at least two sources, a blended "My Rank" column appears as the very first ranking column on the Free Agents page. Your composite members are grouped together with a visual separator so you can still see how the individual rankings compare to your custom blend.

It's worth noting that this is a weighted average, not a popularity contest. If you give KeepTradeCut 3x weight and everything else 1x, KTC's rankings will dominate your composite. Adjust accordingly — or don't, and blame the algorithm when your auction bids don't work out.
imageAlt: Import Rankings page showing ranking source selection with weight controls for building a My Rank composite

title: What's New Blog Redesign
summary: Browse updates with category filters, full detail pages, and a timeline sidebar.
description: The What's New page has received the kind of makeover usually reserved for a franchise quarterback getting a new helmet design — except this one is actually functional. The original flat list of updates was fine when there were five entries, but with the feature count now pushing thirty, it had all the navigability of a phone book.

The redesign introduces category filter tabs so you can zero in on New Pages, New Features, or Enhancements without scrolling past bug fixes you don't care about. Every entry now has its own dedicated detail page with a full description, category badge, and prev/next navigation for the chronological browsers among us. On desktop, a sticky timeline sidebar with color-coded dots lets you jump to any month's updates instantly.

Entries you haven't seen since your last visit are marked with a NEW badge, which is either a helpful convenience or a guilt trip depending on how often you check in. The system tracks your last visit via cookie, so clearing your browser data means everything looks new again — a fresh start, if you need one.
imageAlt: What's New page with category filter tabs, featured entry cards, and timeline sidebar

title: Salary Archive
summary: Browse and download every historical salary spreadsheet from 2009 to 2025.
description: For seventeen years, the league's salary data lived in a scattered archipelago of Google Sheets, each one lovingly maintained by hand and shared via group chat links that expired approximately thirty seconds after being posted. Finding the 2016 salary averages required the kind of archaeological dig usually reserved for ancient civilizations.

The Salary Archive page puts every historical Tagged Salary Averages spreadsheet from 2009 to 2025 in one place, each year linking directly to the original file. It's the complete record — from the league's earliest days of manual spreadsheet management through the transition to automated MFL API data. The Salary Analytics page also now includes an Export to Excel button that generates a multi-sheet workbook with one tab per position plus a Position Summary overview, so you can take current data with you.

Whether you're settling a debate about what quarterbacks cost in 2014 or building a historical model for your auction strategy, the data is now exactly one click away instead of buried in someone's Google Drive.
imageAlt: Salary Archive page listing historical salary spreadsheets from 2009 to 2025

title: Ranking Source in Roster Column
summary: The roster rank column now displays which ranking source is being used instead of a generic label.
description: A small change with outsized quality-of-life impact: the rank column on the Roster page now actually tells you which ranking source it's displaying. Previously, the column header just said "Rank" — leaving you to either remember which source you'd selected, squint at the numbers and try to reverse-engineer it, or simply accept the mystery rankings on faith.

The header now shows the source abbreviation directly — Sleep, FBG, KTC, CBS, and so on — with the full source name revealed on hover. It's the kind of thing you didn't know you needed until you found yourself staring at a rank column wondering if those were dynasty ranks or redraft ranks and whether you should be panicking about your WR3 or not.
imageAlt: Roster page showing ranking source abbreviation in the column header

title: Salary Analytics
summary: Explore salary benchmarks by position with starter averages, medians, and full player breakdowns.
description: The Salary Benchmarks page has been promoted to Salary Analytics, and the upgrade is roughly equivalent to going from a box score to a full analytics department. Every position now displays starter average and starter median based on realistic league starter counts — not just the overall average that gets dragged down by the deep bench players making league minimum.

Browse the complete ranked salary list for any position with a Show More button to reveal the full depth chart of spending. The year selector lets you compare salary trends across every season back to 2021, and weekly snapshot switching shows how values shifted throughout the year — useful for tracking how that mid-season blockbuster trade reshaped the market.

The addition of the Export to Excel button means you can now download a multi-sheet workbook with one tab per position for whatever offline analysis your inner data scientist demands. For the owners who built their entire draft strategy in Excel — you know who you are — this one's for you.
imageAlt: Salary Analytics page with position benchmarks, starter averages, and player salary breakdowns

title: Import Rankings
summary: Import player rankings from FantasyPros, FootballGuys, Sleeper, and more — then drag to set your preferred display order.
description: In a league where information is currency, the Import Rankings page is essentially a money printer. Seven major fantasy football ranking sites — FantasyPros, FootballGuys, Dynasty League Football, KeepTradeCut, Sleeper, Yahoo, and CBS Sports — can now feed their rankings directly into the league's tools using simple browser bookmarklets.

The process is deliberately low-tech and high-trust: drag a bookmarklet to your browser's bookmarks bar, visit your ranking site of choice, click the bookmarklet, and the data flows in. Once imported, drag and drop your saved rankings to control which columns appear and in what order on the Free Agents page. Up to eight ranking columns display for free agents, or five when rostered players are included.

Every byte of ranking data stays private in your browser's local storage — never sent to the server, never shared with other league members. Your scouting advantage is yours alone, which is exactly how it should be in a league where pre-auction intelligence can be worth millions in cap space.
imageAlt: Import Rankings page with bookmarklet instructions and draggable ranking source list

title: Free Agent Scouting Tool
summary: Research free agents with ADP rankings, snap counts, and detailed player profiles.
description: Auction prep used to involve twelve browser tabs, three spreadsheets, and the quiet resignation that you'd never have all the data in one place. The Free Agent Scouting Tool consolidates the essentials into a single sortable table: ADP Redraft rankings, ADP Dynasty rankings, and NFL snap count data from last season — including total snaps and snap percentage — all side by side.

Filter by position, search by name or NFL team, and toggle rookies in or out depending on whether you're feeling bold or cautious this offseason. Every column is sortable, which means you can find the high-usage sleepers — lots of snaps, low ADP — or confirm your suspicion that the player everyone's hyping barely saw the field last year.

This is the pre-auction research hub the league has needed since the first time someone overpaid for a player whose snap count could fit on a Post-it note. Use it before the auction, or don't — and then use it after the auction to figure out where things went wrong.
imageAlt: Free Agents scouting table with player rankings, snap counts, and position filters

title: What's New Page
summary: Track every new feature and improvement in one place.
description: New features used to be announced in the group chat, where they'd enjoy approximately forty-five seconds of visibility before being buried under an avalanche of trade proposals, injury debates, and whatever meme someone found that morning. The What's New page is the league's official changelog — a permanent, browsable record of every feature and improvement as it ships.

Each entry includes a date, description, and a direct link to the relevant page so you can try new features immediately instead of wondering where they live. It's the difference between hearing about a new restaurant and being handed the address, the menu, and a reservation.
imageAlt: What's New changelog page showing feature entries with dates and category badges

title: GM/Coach Mode Toggle
summary: Switch between GM and Coach views on your roster page.
description: This one started with a simple request from The Dream: he wanted to see the top scores from every player. What he got was an entire second perspective on the roster page. The GM tab was always there — salaries, contracts, escalation projections, trade value — but not everyone opens the roster page thinking about cap space. Some people open it on Sunday morning wondering who to start.

The Coach tab strips away the front office and replaces it with what matters on game day. Player averages, opponent matchups, injury designations, Vegas lines, over/unders, and even stadium weather — everything a coach needs to set a lineup without wading through contract details. GM mode is still one click away for when the trade itch hits, but Coach mode is for the people who just want to win this week.

Coach mode is available to all users, with a demo experience for visitors who want to kick the tires. It's accessible directly via URL so anyone who's already decided which hat they're wearing today can skip straight to the view they need.
imageAlt: Roster page in Coach Mode showing matchup data and start/sit columns

title: Trade Bait Marketplace
summary: See which players teams have listed as available for trade.
description: The trade deadline is approaching, and the eternal question echoes across the league: "Who's available?" Previously, answering that question required individually messaging every owner — a process roughly as efficient as going door-to-door asking if anyone wants to sell their house. The Trade Bait Marketplace eliminates the detective work by surfacing every player that teams have flagged as available for trade, all in one view.

Built directly into the Trade Builder, the marketplace shows trade bait across all sixteen teams, filterable by position. During the February transition period — when the league straddles two league years — trade bait from both the current and incoming seasons is displayed so you're working with the full picture.
See a player you like? You're already in the Trade Builder. Start dragging.
imageAlt: Trade Builder page showing the Trade Bait Marketplace with available players

title: Weekly Fixes & Polish (Feb 10-16)
summary: 17 bug fixes and style improvements across navigation & routing, calendar, league summary, rosters, and more.
description: This week's 17 bug fixes and polish improvements touched several areas of the site:
Navigation & Routing — The site is now accessible at theleague.us with proper domain routing, forum proxy, and root redirect. GM Central link updated to use the correct www.theleague.us domain.

Calendar — Calendar now uses the correct calendar year with a previous-year option and swapped category colors. NFL Draft and Season Start events now use the NFL logo icon with multi-color SVG support. Rookie draft date corrected to Saturday after NFL Draft week. Auction results link fixed to the correct MFL module. Single-day event deadlines now default to 8:45 PM PT.
League Summary — Year range now correctly shows 5 years starting from the current league year (2026-2030). Quartile indicators switched from background colors to text colors for better readability.

Rosters — Veteran extension table columns consolidated to prevent overflow on smaller screens. Draft picks card no longer goes missing on the Next Year view after league year rollover. Demo mode opponent averages and ranks now vary per player for realistic sorting.
Trade Builder — React integration added for Trade Builder components to resolve rendering issues.
Playoffs — Playoffs description corrected from 6 to 7 teams to match actual league rules.
Homepage — Hero banner CTA button alignment fixed on mobile. Mobile alignment and padding improved across homepage and What's New page.
Other — Build failure when MFL API is unavailable during offseason now handled gracefully. CSS class scoping fixed in icon sprite. Icon gallery names display single-line with hidden scrollbar.

title: League Summary Page
summary: Compare all 16 teams across cap, roster, and contract metrics.
description: Trying to compare all sixteen teams used to require the patience of a saint and the memory of an elephant. Open one roster page, jot down some numbers, open another, compare, forget what the first team's cap situation looked like, go back, repeat until your browser has more tabs than your salary cap has dollars. The League Summary page ends this madness with a single dashboard.

Fourteen categories of competitive intelligence — salary cap usage, dead money, contract length distribution, draft capital, roster age, extension candidates, and more — are laid out across all sixteen teams in one sortable, scannable view. Five years of historical data lets you track which teams are building for the future and which ones are mortgaging it.
This is the page you open when someone in the group chat says their team is in great cap shape. Trust, but verify.
imageAlt: League Summary dashboard comparing all 16 teams across salary cap and roster metrics

title: Trade Builder
summary: Simulate trades and instantly see salary cap impact for both teams.
description: This one's for Wabbit. The league's most prolific trader — a true fantasy football day trader — used to evaluate every deal the old-fashioned way: fire up a spreadsheet, manually look up salaries and contract lengths for both sides, calculate escalation percentages by hand, and hope nobody fat-fingered a decimal point. If anyone was going to stress-test a trade tool into existence, it was going to be the owner who treats the trade wire like a full-time job.

Select any two teams, drag players and draft picks between them, and watch the salary cap impact update in real time. The tool automatically calculates multi-year cap projections with the league's 10% annual escalation, because nobody wants to discover three years from now that a deal they loved in February is eating their cap alive. By default, it suggests the two teams with the most cap room — on the theory that cap-rich teams make the most willing trade partners — but you can switch to any matchup in the league.

Mock drafts let you rehearse draft night before it happens. The Trade Builder does the same thing for trades — run as many scenarios as you want, see exactly whether the cap math works, and walk into the negotiation already knowing the answer. No more spreadsheets, no more guessing, no more "I think that works under the cap." Early reports suggest Wabbit's trade volume is up roughly 30% since launch, which is either a ringing endorsement or a warning sign depending on whether you're in his division.
imageAlt: Trade Builder showing two-team player selection with salary cap impact analysis

title: What's Next Timeline
summary: The homepage now shows your current, next, and upcoming league events.
description: The homepage now answers the question that used to require checking the league calendar, the group chat, and occasionally a league member's surprisingly accurate memory: "What's happening next?" A three-card timeline at the top of the page shows the current event, the next upcoming event, and the one after that — each with countdown timers and direct action links.

The cards update automatically as events pass, so the page always reflects the current state of the league calendar. During busy periods like the offseason, where tagging windows, free agency, and the draft can overlap, this becomes the single fastest way to know what you should be paying attention to right now.

It's personalized to your team where applicable, so links point to your specific roster or franchise tools rather than generic league pages. The days of missing a deadline because you forgot to check the calendar are — theoretically — over.
imageAlt: Homepage showing the What's Next timeline with three upcoming league event cards

title: League Calendar
summary: View all important league dates and deadlines in one place.
description: Every league event for the season, in one place, color-coded and countdown-enabled. The League Calendar page displays tagging periods, free agency windows, draft dates, the trading deadline, playoff rounds, and every other date that matters — organized by category with visual differentiation so you can scan for the events relevant to your current planning horizon.

Events include countdown timers that tell you exactly how many days until the next deadline, action links to the relevant league tools, and status badges indicating whether something is active, upcoming, or urgent. A year selector lets you browse the previous and upcoming seasons' calendars for long-term planning.
This is the page you bookmark and check every Monday morning. Or you can keep relying on group chat reminders that inevitably arrive twelve hours after the deadline. Your call.
imageAlt: League Calendar page with color-coded events and countdown timers

title: ESPN Player Headshots
summary: Player cards now show real ESPN headshot photos.
description: Player cards across the site now display actual human faces instead of the featureless silhouette placeholders that made every roster look like a witness protection program. Real ESPN headshot photos have been integrated across all player-facing components, displayed at 150% zoom for a clean, cropped presentation.

It's a cosmetic upgrade, yes, but there's something to be said for being able to visually identify the players you're investing millions of cap dollars in. The system falls back gracefully to MFL photos when ESPN data isn't available, and to a default placeholder as a last resort — so even the most obscure practice squad player gets some kind of representation.

title: Custom Football Icon Set
summary: A complete set of custom-designed football icons throughout the app.
description: The navigation drawer and header have been outfitted with a complete set of custom-designed SVG football icons, replacing the generic icon library that made the app look like it could have been about anything from project management to recipe tracking. The new icons include purpose-built designs for lineups, trades, drafts, standings, salary management, and more.

It's a small touch that adds up to a significantly more cohesive experience. When you click on the trades icon, it looks like it belongs in a football league, not a stock portfolio app. The entire set is built as an SVG sprite for fast loading, which means you get better visuals with less overhead — the rare upgrade that doesn't cost you any cap space.

title: Navigation Drawer Redesign
summary: A new collapsible side drawer for faster navigation between pages.
description: The old top navigation bar served the league faithfully for its era, but with the page count growing faster than a rookie's contract demands, something more scalable was in order. The new navigation drawer is a full slide-out panel with grouped sections, league switching, and team information — think of it as the difference between a folding map and a GPS.

On desktop, the drawer pushes page content aside when open and collapses to a slim icon rail when you need more screen space. On mobile, it overlays as a full-screen panel with a dimmed backdrop. Keyboard navigation, focus trapping, and Escape-to-close are all built in for the power users who refuse to touch a mouse.

The drawer footer shows your team's logo, owner name, and franchise info — a small personalization that serves as a constant reminder of which dynasty you're building. Or, depending on your roster, which dynasty you're rebuilding.

title: Weekly Fixes & Polish (Jan 17-Feb 8)
summary: 14 bug fixes and style improvements across navigation, rosters, design system, and more.
description: Three weeks of bug fixes and polish from mid-January through early February:

Navigation — Navigation drawer UI polished with updated shadows, link ordering, and removing extra shadows. Switch Leagues option now only visible when you have teams in both leagues. Mobile toggle and spacing fixes.

Rosters & Coach Mode — Coach mode live scoring fixed. Add/drop page colors corrected. Opponent average column aligned properly. Coach mode missing data issue resolved. Roster data now updates on each build.

Design System — New box shadows applied site-wide for depth consistency. Bold college names and alternating row backgrounds added to tables. Page animations refined. Scoreboard colors corrected. Footer now uses flexbox to always sit at the bottom of the page.
Infrastructure — Bundle size checks added to prevent Vercel deploy failures. Roster sync push race condition prevented. Roster sync schedule changed from every 5 minutes to every 20 minutes.

title: Weekly Fixes & Polish (Jan 1-12)
summary: 12 bug fixes and style improvements across playoffs, rosters, auction predictor, and more.
description: Two weeks of bug fixes and polish from the new year:

Playoffs & Draft — Champion draft picks can now be traded via the futureDraftPicks API. Champion picks no longer re-added after merge. Playoff bracket data loading made more robust with better fallbacks.

Rosters — Season year now uses the PUBLIC_MFL_YEAR env var consistently across all pages. Chatmaster banner fixed. Draft history corrected. Compensatory picks added to draft view. Draft pick color corrected.
Auction Predictor — Fixed 500 error on Vercel caused by file path issue. Projected view now uses sortedPredictions correctly.
Design System — Box shadow inconsistencies cleaned up across homepage and borders. Team icon no longer cut off in navigation.

title: Weekly Fixes & Polish (Dec 22-28)
summary: 15 bug fixes and style improvements across playoffs, navigation, mobile, and more.
description: Holiday week polish covering playoffs, navigation, and mobile experience:
Playoffs — Champion logic updated for accurate bracket progression. AFL bracket fixes applied. Conference and division names corrected. NIT seeding and seed font size adjusted. Playoff icons fixed.
Navigation — Header/footer logic refined for consistent layout. Mobile view cleaned up across prize page and playoff brackets. Remove unnecessary links for AFL.

Mobile — Mobile styles fixed for TheLeague pages. Off-white background color adjusted to be Mac-friendly. Apple-safe background colors applied. Franchise icon resizing improved. Mobile padding fixed on AFL playoff page.
Other — Team nav fixes applied. Draft predictor updated for AFL support. Live data endpoints added for real-time playoff scoring.

title: Weekly Fixes & Polish (Dec 8-21)
summary: 18 bug fixes and style improvements across playoffs, MVP, mobile, and more.
description: Two weeks of polish covering live scoring, playoffs, and mobile improvements:

Playoffs & Scoring — Live scoring links added from playoff page. Live scoring indicator and progress bar implemented. Bracket data kept fresh with frequent updates. Playoff data now uses predictive fallback when live data is unavailable.
MVP & Analytics — All-time MVP tracking added. Team of the Year support introduced. San Diego Chargers historical data now included.
Mobile — Mobile menu fix for header. Rosters layout fixed on mobile. Footer links corrected. Draft predictor now shows on roster page. Icons enlarged for better tap targets.

Other — Standings data freshness improved. Roster rotation implemented. Playoff links back to MFL added. Sunday ticket updates now show both broadcast windows. Timezone and calendar icon updates. URL shareability improved.

title: Weekly Fixes & Polish (Dec 1-7)
summary: 10 bug fixes and style improvements across AFL setup, infrastructure, and builds.
description: First week of December focused on AFL Fantasy support and infrastructure:

AFL Fantasy — AFL theme styles and build script added. Division standings rules implemented. Both leagues now update independently. Salary processing correctly skipped for AFL. Icons enabled across AFL pages.

Infrastructure — Vercel config simplified to run Astro build directly. Asset domain updated to use correct Vercel deployment URL. Incorrect monorepo Vercel config removed. Syncing workflow split by league for reliability.
Other — pnpm lockfile refreshed. JS build system for MFL site established.

title: Weekly Fixes & Polish (Nov 24-30)
summary: 16 bug fixes and style improvements across standings, navigation, draft predictor, and more.
description: Thanksgiving week polish across navigation, standings, and draft tools:
Standings — Standings UI updates with tiebreaker improvements. Tab icons fixed. Mobile height adjusted on standings page. Larger table fonts for readability.
Navigation — Navigation updates across the site. Breadcrumbs refined. Slim breadcrumb and logo layout. Mobile header size updated. Footer updates applied. Next year summary links added.
Draft — Draft predictor UI cleaned up with better team name display. Year selector updated. Draft history fixed on mobile. Header link corrected.

Trade — Trade bait API endpoint corrected. Player ID type mismatch fixed when checking trade bait set. Trade bait display renamed to 'Trade Block' per league terminology. Trade block indicator repositioned.
Other — Ninja banner updated. Fix icon size on mobile. Fix draft history on mobile.

title: Weekly Fixes & Polish (Nov 17-23)
summary: 20 bug fixes and style improvements across rosters, salary, charts, login, and more.
description: A busy week of polish as core pages launched:

Rosters — Roster layout changes for better readability. Defense display fixed. NFL logo corrected. Player count display added. Hamburger menu and roster cleanup. Next year 2-column layout aligned. Player duplicates resolved.
Salary & Contracts — Extension calculations fixed. Franchise tag updates applied. Franchise eligibility logic corrected. Cap space color fixed. Salary formula corrected.
Charts — Donut charts now include legends. New bar chart added with proper scale. Position pie chart introduced. Better visual charts across the site.
Login & Auth — Session cookie now sent correctly in development with SameSite=Lax. Auto-select logged-in user's team on roster page. User menu with login modal added to header.
Other — Header and team nav fixed. NFL images updated. Player modal with injury details improved. College analysis max-width on NFL logo fixed.

title: Weekly Fixes & Polish (Nov 9-16)
summary: 18 bug fixes and style improvements across live scoring, submit lineup, standings, and more.
description: First full week of polish as the site took shape:
Live Scoring — Live scoring fixes across multiple pages. Better live scoring display. Fix live scoring page exclusion for custom scripts. Live scoring bug fixes.
Submit Lineup — Submit lineup page styling improved. Scroll bar and lineup updates. Fix bottom of submit lineup page. Submit caption margin issue fixed.
Standings — Standings updates and alignment. New button style applied. Better icon support added. Standings tweaks for readability.
Message Board — Custom message board CSS added. Ad blocker support improved. Ads removed from message board. Message board post alignment fixed.
Mobile — Mobile view fixes across pages. Power rank page updated. Add/drop page icon preserved. Fix mobile responsiveness throughout.
Other — Updated to latest Sass. Footer fix. Center cards on asset pages. Icon sizes standardized.

title: League Rules & Constitution
summary: The full league constitution is now available on the site.
description: Every league rules argument since the beginning of time has followed the same pattern: someone makes a claim, someone else disputes it, and then everyone spends twenty minutes searching the group chat for the PDF that may or may not contain the relevant clause. The Rules & Constitution page puts the entire league constitution online in a searchable, browsable format — scoring settings, roster requirements, contract details, tagging rules, and everything else that governs how this league operates.

Twenty-eight anchor-linked sections cover everything from salary cap mechanics and the 10% escalation rule to compensatory pick formulas and playoff payout structures. It's the kind of comprehensive reference that makes you wonder how the league functioned without it — and the answer is: barely, with a lot of scrolling through old messages.

The next time someone in the group chat asks "what's the franchise tag rule again?" — just send them the link. It's faster, it's accurate, and it's significantly more satisfying than typing out a three-paragraph explanation that gets immediately buried under more questions.
imageAlt: League Rules and Constitution page with searchable rule sections

title: Playoff Brackets
summary: View the full playoff bracket with matchups, results, and prizes.
description: Playoff brackets have arrived, and they're doing considerably more than just showing you who plays whom. The bracket page displays the full tournament structure — winners bracket, consolation rounds, and the ever-dignified Toilet Bowl — with live scoring that auto-refreshes every sixty seconds during game windows.

Each matchup shows team scores with progress bars indicating remaining player time, status indicators for Scheduled, Live, and Final states, and winner flags once the dust settles. The Prizes tab breaks down the full payout structure from the championship down through sixth place, plus the weekly high scores that have been accumulating since week one.

On mobile, the brackets use snap scrolling with navigation indicators so you can swipe through rounds without losing your place. The page auto-scrolls to the current week's matchups, because nobody opens the playoff bracket to reminisce about round one — they open it to see if they're still alive.
imageAlt: Playoff bracket page showing tournament matchups and winners bracket

title: Draft Order Predictor
summary: See the projected draft order for next season based on current standings.
description: Draft order in this league is determined by a combination of regular season finishing position and Toilet Bowl results, which means figuring out where you're picking next year requires either a complex flowchart or this page. The Draft Order Predictor automatically calculates projected pick positions using current standings, playoff outcomes, and the league's draft order rules.

The page updates throughout the season, so you can track your projected pick position as the standings shift. Historical views let you look back at past drafts, including which picks were traded and where they originally belonged. It's the planning tool every rebuilding team needs — not that anyone in this league would ever tank intentionally, of course.

For the playoff-bound teams, the predictor is a useful reminder of what your late-round pick will look like in draft capital. For the teams fighting for Toilet Bowl positioning, it's a roadmap to a better future. Either way, knowledge is power.
imageAlt: Draft Order Predictor showing projected pick order based on current standings

title: Standings Page
summary: View current standings with division, playoff, and all-play views.
description: MFL provides standings data, but presenting it in a way that doesn't require squinting at a table designed in 2003 was overdue. The Standings page offers three distinct views: division standings showing how the four divisions shake out, a league-wide playoff race ranking all sixteen teams, and all-play records revealing how each team would have fared against every opponent every week.

Each view includes win-loss records, points for and against, and relevant tiebreaker details. The year selector lets you browse historical standings to revisit past glory or confirm that, yes, that season really was as bad as you remember. Your team is highlighted for quick identification, because finding yourself in a list of sixteen should not require more than zero seconds.

The all-play view deserves special mention as the great equalizer: it strips away schedule luck and shows which teams were genuinely good and which ones just happened to face the right opponents at the right time. Prepare for uncomfortable truths.
imageAlt: Standings page showing division standings with win-loss records and points

title: Team Rosters
summary: View all team rosters with player details, salaries, and contracts.
description: This is the page where it all starts and where most decisions end. The Roster page displays every team's complete player inventory with salary, contract length, position, and status. Next-year salary projections with the 10% annual escalation are calculated automatically, because knowing what a player costs today is only half the picture — knowing what they'll cost tomorrow is what separates contenders from cap casualties.

The page integrates salary cap summaries, dead money calculations, cap space projections, and veteran extension candidate identification — essentially packaging the work of a front office analyst into a single view. Player detail modals provide deep dives into individual contracts, and draft picks are tracked alongside roster players to give a complete picture of team assets.

Whitside got tired of loading the page and seeing Computer Jock's Chiseled Physique every time, so the default is now a random team. If you're logged in through My Fantasy League, the page automatically loads your team. If you're not logged in, you'll see a different team each visit — which is either a fun surprise or a rude awakening, depending on whose roster comes up. Every other tool in the app — the Trade Builder, the Salary Analytics page, the Coach tab — ultimately feeds information back to decisions made here. Consider it home base.
imageAlt: Team Roster page showing player salaries, contract lengths, and position details

title: Salary History Charts
summary: Track how team salary spending has changed across seasons.
description: Salary trends in a dynasty league are like tides: if you're not tracking them, you're going to get caught. The Salary History page provides interactive charts showing how franchise tag averages and extension averages have moved across seasons for every position group — QB, RB, WR, TE, PK, and DEF.

Toggle between franchise tag history (top 3 salary averages) and extension history (top 5 averages) to see which positions are inflating fastest. The multi-season view lets you overlay years to spot the trends that inform your next contract decision — like the fact that wide receiver salaries have been climbing at a rate that should concern anyone with three of them on long-term deals.

This is auction intelligence in chart form. Knowing that running back salaries dipped last year might mean value. Knowing that quarterback salaries have risen every year for five straight years means you should plan accordingly. Or ignore the data entirely and bid with your heart — the page will still be here when you want to understand what went wrong.
imageAlt: Salary History page with interactive charts showing cap spending trends across seasons

title: Salary-Adjusted MVP Rankings
summary: See which players deliver the most value relative to their salary.
description: In a salary cap league, raw fantasy points are only half the equation. A player scoring 250 points on a $10M contract is valuable. The same player scoring 250 points on a $1M contract is a league-altering asset. The Salary-Adjusted MVP Rankings capture this distinction by ranking players on efficiency: points produced per dollar spent.

The rankings cover every position and surface the players delivering production that far exceeds their contract value — the kind of players you build dynasties around. Position-specific leaderboards let you drill into which quarterbacks, running backs, and receivers are carrying their weight relative to their cap hit.

The next time someone brags about their star player's point total, check the MVP page. The real winner might be the owner whose roster is full of players nobody wanted at prices nobody else was willing to match.
imageAlt: MVP Rankings page showing salary-adjusted value rankings for top performers

title: Salary Analytics
summary: Compare your team's spending against league averages by position.
description: Understanding what your team spends by position is useful. Understanding what your team spends by position compared to what everyone else spends is a competitive advantage. The Salary Benchmarks page provides position-by-position spending comparisons against league-wide averages, making it immediately clear where you're overpaying and where you've found value.

Each position group shows where your spending falls relative to the league average, turning abstract salary numbers into actionable intelligence. If you're 40% above the league average at quarterback and 30% below at running back, that tells a story about your roster construction — and possibly about your upcoming auction strategy.

This was the first salary analysis tool built for the league, and it has since grown into the full Salary Analytics suite. But the core question it answers hasn't changed: are you getting your money's worth?
imageAlt: Salary Benchmarks page comparing team spending against league averages by position

title: Extension Calculator
summary: Calculate the future cost of contract extensions with 10% annual escalation.
description: The 10% annual salary escalation is the silent killer of dynasty rosters. A $2M contract looks brilliant until you realize it becomes $2.2M next year, $2.42M the year after, and $2.66M the year after that. The Extension Calculator did this math for you across multiple extension scenarios, projecting costs for 2, 3, and 4-year extensions with the escalation baked in.

We say "did" because this page has already been retired. Things move fast around here. The extension calculator is now built directly into the Roster page, where it automatically surfaces the top five extension candidates and top three franchise tag candidates for every team, live, with no extra clicks required. Sit back down on your rocking chair — by the time you stood up to use the standalone calculator, it had already been absorbed into something better.

The original concept remains intact: know what an extension will cost before you offer it, not after. The difference between a 3-year and 4-year deal might look small in year one, but the escalation compounds. The calculator just lives on the Roster page now, where it belongs.
imageAlt: Extension Calculator showing projected contract costs with 10% annual escalation

title: Asset Library
summary: Browse team logos, banners, and brand graphics for all 16 teams.
description: This league has always had extremely attractive logos and banners. That was never the problem. The problem was that they lived in Discord messages, email attachments, and the occasional blurry screenshot that someone insisted was "close enough." The Asset Library is a page that exists for one reason and one reason only: to show you the logos and banners.

Logos, banners, icons, and even PSD source files are organized by team with search and division-based filtering. There are no salary calculations here. No escalation projections. No trade scenarios. Just extremely attractive logos and banners, available in one click instead of buried in a chat thread from 2019.
Because if you're going to mock someone's team, at least use the right logo.
imageAlt: Asset Library gallery showing team logos, banners, and brand graphics
